title: Der wunderliche Spielmann
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 8. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The eighth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 8), a wry little story of a lonely fiddler whose playing lures the wolf, the fox, and the hare, each of whom he tricks and cruelly traps — the wolf’s forepaws pinned in a split oak, the fox flung up between two saplings, the hare wound fast to a tree — because it is human company, not beasts, that he wants. When a woodcutter finally comes, the fiddler is glad, and the raised axe sends the freed animals fleeing. The translation is drawn straight from the 1857 text and keeps its dark, unbowdlerized turns. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, giengen, fieng, aufgieng, thun, Thiere, Wuth, daß, mußt and ward.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein wunderlicher Spielmann, der gieng durch einen Wald mutterselig allein und dachte hin und her, und als für seine Gedanken nichts mehr übrig war, sprach er zu sich selbst „mir wird hier im Walde Zeit und Weile lang, ich will einen guten Gesellen herbei holen.“
sentences.l: There was once a strange musician, who went through a wood utterly alone and thought this way and that, and when for his thoughts nothing more was left over, he spoke to himself, “time and while grow long for me here in the wood; I will fetch myself a good companion here.”
sentences.i: There was once a strange fiddler, who was walking through a wood all alone, his thoughts wandering this way and that; and when he had nothing left to think about, he said to himself, “Time hangs heavy on me here in the wood; I will fetch myself a good companion.”
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ “mutterselig allein” is an intensive idiom, ‘utterly, forlornly alone.’ “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “mir wird die Zeit und Weile lang” = ‘time hangs heavy on me’ (an impersonal dative, mir).

sentences.t: Da nahm er die Geige vom Rücken und fidelte eins daß es durch die Bäume schallte.
sentences.l: Then he took the fiddle from his back and fiddled one, so that it resounded through the trees.
sentences.i: Then he took the fiddle from his back and struck up a tune, so that it rang out through the trees.
sentences.n: “fidelte eins” — ‘fiddled one [tune]’; eins stands for ein Stück, a set colloquial way of saying ‘played a piece.’ You will meet this same “eins” again at the very end (“spielte ... noch eins”). “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Nicht lange, so kam ein Wolf durch das Dickicht daher getrabt.
sentences.l: Not long, and there came a wolf trotting along through the thicket.
sentences.i: Before long a wolf came trotting up through the thicket.
sentences.n: “Nicht lange, so ...” = ‘it was not long before ...’ “kam ... daher getrabt” — a verb of motion (kommen) plus a past participle (getrabt, from traben) is the normal German way to say ‘came trotting’; you will see it again with geschlichen (slinking) and gesprungen (bounding).

sentences.t: „Ach, ein Wolf kommt! nach dem trage ich kein Verlangen,“ sagte der Spielmann: aber der Wolf schritt näher und sprach zu ihm „ei, du lieber Spielmann, was fidelst du so schön! das möcht ich auch lernen.“
sentences.l: “Ah, a wolf is coming! for that one I bear no longing,” said the musician; but the wolf stepped nearer and spoke to him, “ei, you dear musician, how beautifully you fiddle! that I should like to learn too.”
sentences.i: “Ah, a wolf is coming! I have no desire for him,” said the fiddler; but the wolf stepped closer and said to him, “Why, dear fiddler, how beautifully you play! I should like to learn that too.”
sentences.n: “nach etwas Verlangen tragen” = to feel a longing/desire for something. “möcht” is the contracted möchte (‘should like’), subjunctive of mögen. “ei” is an old exclamation of mild surprise, ‘why!, ah!’

sentences.t: „Das ist bald gelernt,“ antwortete ihm der Spielmann, „du mußt nur alles thun, was ich dich heiße.“
sentences.l: “That is soon learned,” the musician answered him, “you must only do everything that I bid you.”
sentences.i: “That is soon learned,” the fiddler answered him; “you have only to do everything I tell you.”
sentences.n: “mußt” and “thun” are 1857 spellings of musst and tun (thun keeps the old silent -h-). “jemanden etwas heißen” = to bid/command someone (to do) something; note the double accusative in “was ich dich heiße.”

sentences.t: „O Spielmann,“ sprach der Wolf, „ich will dir gehorchen, wie ein Schüler seinem Meister.“
sentences.l: “O musician,” spoke the wolf, “I will obey you, like a pupil his master.”
sentences.n: “gehorchen” takes the dative, so “dir” and “seinem Meister” are datives — to obey *to* you, *to* his master. This whole exchange is repeated almost word for word by the fox and the hare — the folktale rule of three.

sentences.t: Der Spielmann hieß ihn mitgehen, und als sie ein Stück Wegs zusammen gegangen waren, kamen sie an einen alten Eichbaum, der innen hohl und in der Mitte aufgerissen war.
sentences.l: The musician bid him go along, and when they had gone a stretch of way together, they came to an old oak tree that was hollow inside and torn open in the middle.
sentences.i: The fiddler bade him come along, and when they had gone a stretch of the way together, they came to an old oak that was hollow within and split open down the middle.
sentences.n: “ein Stück Wegs” = a stretch of road (Wegs is an old genitive). “aufgerissen” is the past participle of aufreißen (‘to tear open’). This split trunk is the trap to come — keep it in mind.

sentences.t: „Sieh her,“ sprach der Spielmann, „willst du fideln lernen, so lege die Vorderpfoten in diesen Spalt.“
sentences.l: “Look here,” spoke the musician, “if you want to learn to fiddle, then lay your front paws into this cleft.”
sentences.i: “Look here,” said the fiddler, “if you want to learn to fiddle, lay your forepaws in this crack.”
sentences.n: “willst du ..., so ...” — a conditional made by putting the verb first, ‘if you want to ..., then ...’ “lege” is the imperative of legen (‘lay, put’).

sentences.t: Der Wolf gehorchte, aber der Spielmann hob schnell einen Stein auf und keilte ihm die beiden Pfoten mit einem Schlag so fest daß er wie ein Gefangener da liegen bleiben mußte.
sentences.l: The wolf obeyed, but the musician quickly picked up a stone and wedged both his paws fast with one blow, so fast that he had to stay lying there like a prisoner.
sentences.i: The wolf obeyed, but the fiddler quickly picked up a stone and with a single blow wedged both his paws so tight that he had to lie there like a prisoner.
sentences.n: This is the wolf’s painful trap: the fiddler jams a stone into the split so the wolf’s forepaws are pinned. “hob ... auf” is the separable verb aufheben (‘to pick up’). “keilte” (keilen) = to wedge; “ihm ... die Pfoten” uses the dative of the one whose paws they are.

sentences.t: „Warte da so lange bis ich wieder komme,“ sagte der Spielmann und gieng seines Weges.
sentences.l: “Wait there so long until I come again,” said the musician and went his way.
sentences.i: “Wait there until I come back,” said the fiddler, and went on his way.
sentences.n: “seines Weges gehen” = to go one’s way (an old genitive of direction). This whole line — the trapping and the mocking farewell — recurs almost word for word with the fox and the hare.

sentences.t: Über eine Weile sprach er abermals zu sich selber „mir wird hier im Walde Zeit und Weile lang, ich will einen andern Gesellen herbeiholen,“ nahm seine Geige und fidelte wieder in den Wald hinein.
sentences.l: After a while he spoke again to himself, “time and while grow long for me here in the wood; I will fetch another companion here,” took his fiddle and fiddled again into the wood.
sentences.i: After a while he said to himself once more, “Time hangs heavy on me here in the wood; I will fetch another companion,” and he took his fiddle and struck up again into the wood.
sentences.n: “abermals” = once again, an elevated word. “herbeiholen” (‘to fetch here’) is separable but held together as an infinitive. “fidelte ... hinein” — hinein marks the sound going *into* the depths of the wood.

sentences.t: Nicht lange, so kam ein Fuchs durch die Bäume daher geschlichen.
sentences.l: Not long, and there came a fox slinking along through the trees.
sentences.i: Before long a fox came slinking up through the trees.
sentences.n: “geschlichen” is the past participle of schleichen (‘to creep, slink’) — the same “kam ... daher [participle]” pattern as the wolf’s getrabt.

sentences.t: „Ach, ein Fuchs kommt!“ sagte der Spielmann „nach dem trage ich kein Verlangen.“
sentences.l: “Ah, a fox is coming!” said the musician, “for that one I bear no longing.”
sentences.i: “Ah, a fox is coming!” said the fiddler, “I have no desire for him.”

sentences.t: Der Fuchs kam zu ihm heran, und sprach „ei, du lieber Spielmann, was fidelst du so schön! das möcht ich auch lernen.“
sentences.l: The fox came up to him and spoke, “ei, you dear musician, how beautifully you fiddle! that I should like to learn too.”
sentences.i: The fox came up to him and said, “Why, dear fiddler, how beautifully you play! I should like to learn that too.”
sentences.n: “kam ... heran” (herankommen, to come up, approach) is separable. The fox’s words exactly echo the wolf’s — the tale plays out each scene again with the next animal.

sentences.t: „Das ist bald gelernt,“ sprach der Spielmann, „du mußt nur alles thun, was ich dich heiße.“
sentences.l: “That is soon learned,” spoke the musician, “you must only do everything that I bid you.”

sentences.t: „O Spielmann,“ antwortete der Fuchs, „ich will dir gehorchen, wie ein Schüler seinem Meister.“
sentences.l: “O musician,” answered the fox, “I will obey you, like a pupil his master.”

sentences.t: „Folge mir,“ sagte der Spielmann, und als sie ein Stück Wegs gegangen waren, kamen sie auf einen Fußweg, zu dessen beiden Seiten hohe Sträuche standen.
sentences.l: “Follow me,” said the musician, and when they had gone a stretch of way, they came onto a footpath, to whose two sides tall bushes stood.
sentences.i: “Follow me,” said the fiddler, and when they had gone a stretch of the way, they came to a footpath with tall bushes standing on either side.
sentences.n: “Folge” is the imperative of folgen, which takes the dative (“mir”). “zu dessen beiden Seiten” = ‘on both sides of which’ (dessen is the genitive relative pronoun).

sentences.t: Da hielt der Spielmann still, bog von der einen Seite ein Haselnußbäumchen zur Erde herab und trat mit dem Fuß auf die Spitze, dann bog er von der andern Seite noch ein Bäumchen herab und sprach „wohlan, Füchslein, wenn du etwas lernen willst, so reich mir deine linke Vorderpfote.“
sentences.l: There the musician held still, bent a little hazel tree down to the earth from the one side and stepped with his foot on the tip; then he bent another little tree down from the other side and spoke, “well then, little fox, if you want to learn something, then reach me your left front paw.”
sentences.i: There the fiddler stopped, bent a little hazel sapling down to the ground on one side and set his foot on its tip; then he bent down another sapling on the other side and said, “Well then, little fox, if you want to learn something, give me your left forepaw.”
sentences.n: “hielt ... still” (stillhalten, to hold still). “Haselnußbäumchen” and “Füchslein” are diminutives (-chen/-lein): ‘little hazel tree,’ ‘little fox’ — the coaxing tone as he sets the trap. “wohlan” is an archaic ‘well then, come now.’ “reich” is the imperative of reichen (‘to hand, reach’).

sentences.t: Der Fuchs gehorchte und der Spielmann band ihm die Pfote an den linken Stamm.
sentences.l: The fox obeyed and the musician bound his paw to the left trunk.
sentences.i: The fox obeyed, and the fiddler tied his paw to the left sapling.
sentences.n: “band ihm die Pfote” — dative ihm for the fox whose paw it is: he bound the paw *of* the fox.

sentences.t: „Füchslein,“ sprach er, „nun reich mir die rechte:“ die band er ihm an den rechten Stamm.
sentences.l: “Little fox,” spoke he, “now reach me the right one:” that one he bound for him to the right trunk.
sentences.i: “Little fox,” said he, “now give me the right one,” and that one he tied to the right sapling.
sentences.n: “die rechte” = the right one (paw). “die band er” — the object “die” is placed first for emphasis, ‘that one he tied.’

sentences.t: Und als er nachgesehen hatte, ob die Knoten der Stricke auch fest genug waren, ließ er los, und die Bäumchen fuhren in die Höhe und schnellten das Füchslein hinauf daß es in der Luft schwebte und zappelte.
sentences.l: And when he had looked to see whether the knots of the ropes were firm enough too, he let go, and the little trees shot up into the height and jerked the little fox up, so that it hovered and struggled in the air.
sentences.i: And when he had checked whether the knots in the cords were tight enough, he let go, and the saplings sprang upright and flung the little fox aloft, so that it dangled and wriggled in the air.
sentences.n: Here is the fox’s trap: bent between two saplings and flung up to hang in the air. “nachgesehen” (nachsehen, to check) and “ließ ... los” (loslassen, to let go) are separable. “fuhren in die Höhe” = shot upward. “schnellten” (schnellen) = flung, snapped up.

sentences.t: „Warte da so lange bis ich wiederkomme,“ sagte der Spielmann und gieng seines Weges.
sentences.l: “Wait there so long until I come again,” said the musician and went his way.
sentences.i: “Wait there until I come back,” said the fiddler, and went on his way.
sentences.n: The same mocking farewell as to the wolf; here “wiederkomme” is written as one word (wiederkommen).

sentences.t: Wiederum sprach er zu sich „Zeit und Weile wird mir hier im Walde lang; ich will einen andern Gesellen herbei holen,“ nahm seine Geige, und der Klang erschallte durch den Wald.
sentences.l: Again he spoke to himself, “time and while grow long for me here in the wood; I will fetch another companion here,” took his fiddle, and the sound rang out through the wood.
sentences.i: Once more he said to himself, “Time hangs heavy on me here in the wood; I will fetch another companion,” and he took his fiddle, and the sound rang out through the forest.
sentences.n: “Wiederum” = once again. “erschallte” (erschallen) = rang out, resounded. This is the third time the ‘time hangs heavy’ line returns — the folktale’s rule of three, now bringing the hare.

sentences.t: Da kam ein Häschen daher gesprungen.
sentences.l: There came a little hare bounding along.
sentences.i: There a little hare came bounding up.
sentences.n: “Häschen” is the diminutive of Hase (‘hare’). “gesprungen” (springen) completes the “kam ... daher [participle]” motion pattern once more — the hare comes bounding.

sentences.t: „Ach, ein Hase kommt!“ sagte der Spielmann, „den wollte ich nicht haben.“
sentences.l: “Ah, a hare is coming!” said the musician, “that one I did not want to have.”
sentences.i: “Ah, a hare is coming!” said the fiddler, “I didn’t want that one.”
sentences.n: The refrain varies slightly here (“den wollte ich nicht haben” instead of the wolf’s and fox’s “nach dem trage ich kein Verlangen”) — Grimm rarely repeats a formula quite exactly.

sentences.t: „Ei, du lieber Spielmann,“ sagte das Häschen, „was fidelst du so schön, das möchte ich auch lernen.“
sentences.l: “Ei, you dear musician,” said the little hare, “how beautifully you fiddle, that I should like to learn too.”
sentences.i: “Why, dear fiddler,” said the little hare, “how beautifully you play — I should like to learn that too.”
sentences.n: Here the full “möchte” is written out (the wolf and fox had the contracted “möcht”).

sentences.t: „Das ist bald gelernt,“ sprach der Spielmann, „du mußt nur alles thun was ich dich heiße.“
sentences.l: “That is soon learned,” spoke the musician, “you must only do everything that I bid you.”

sentences.t: „O Spielmann,“ antwortete das Häslein, „ich will dir gehorchen wie ein Schüler seinem Meister.“
sentences.l: “O musician,” answered the little hare, “I will obey you like a pupil his master.”
sentences.n: “Häslein” is another diminutive of Hase, alternating with Häschen in this tale — the -lein and -chen endings are freely interchangeable.

sentences.t: Sie giengen ein Stück Wegs zusammen, bis sie zu einer lichten Stelle im Wald kamen, wo ein Espenbaum stand.
sentences.l: They went a stretch of way together, until they came to a bright open spot in the wood, where an aspen tree stood.
sentences.i: They went a stretch of the way together, until they came to an open clearing in the wood, where an aspen stood.
sentences.n: “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen. “eine lichte Stelle” = a bright, open spot, a clearing. “Espenbaum” = aspen tree.

sentences.t: Der Spielmann band dem Häschen einen langen Bindfaden um den Hals, wovon er das andere Ende an den Baum knüpfte.
sentences.l: The musician bound the little hare a long string around its neck, of which he knotted the other end to the tree.
sentences.i: The fiddler tied a long string around the little hare’s neck, and knotted the other end to the tree.
sentences.n: “band dem Häschen ... um den Hals” — dative dem Häschen for whose neck it is. “wovon” = ‘of which,’ a relative adverb (wo- + von). “knüpfte” (knüpfen) = knotted, tied.

sentences.t: „Munter, Häschen, jetzt spring mir zwanzigmal um den Baum herum,“ rief der Spielmann, und das Häschen gehorchte, und wie es zwanzigmal herumgelaufen war, so hatte sich der Bindfaden zwanzigmal um den Stamm gewickelt, und das Häschen war gefangen, und es mochte ziehen und zerren wie es wollte, es schnitt sich nur den Faden in den weichen Hals.
sentences.l: “Lively now, little hare, spring around the tree twenty times for me,” cried the musician, and the little hare obeyed, and when it had run around twenty times, the string had wound itself twenty times around the trunk, and the little hare was caught, and it might pull and tug as it would, it only cut the cord into its own soft neck.
sentences.i: “Come, little hare, now hop round the tree twenty times for me,” cried the fiddler, and the little hare obeyed; and when it had run round twenty times, the string had wound twenty times around the trunk, and the little hare was caught; and pull and tug as it might, it only cut the cord deeper into its own soft neck.
sentences.n: The hare’s trap: tied to the tree, it winds itself fast by circling. “munter” = lively, brisk (an encouraging ‘come now!’). “spring mir” has an ethical dative mir (‘just spring for me’). “zwanzigmal” = twenty times. “es mochte ... wie es wollte” = ‘however much it might ...’; “schnitt sich ... den Faden in den Hals” — the reflexive sich shows it cut the cord into its *own* neck.

sentences.t: „Warte da so lange bis ich wiederkomme,“ sprach der Spielmann und gieng weiter.
sentences.l: “Wait there so long until I come again,” spoke the musician and went further.
sentences.i: “Wait there until I come back,” said the fiddler, and went on.
sentences.n: The mocking farewell for the third and last time.

sentences.t: Der Wolf indessen hatte gerückt, gezogen, an dem Stein gebissen, und so lange gearbeitet, bis er die Pfoten frei gemacht und wieder aus der Spalte gezogen hatte.
sentences.l: The wolf meanwhile had shoved, pulled, bitten at the stone, and worked so long until he had made his paws free and pulled them out of the cleft again.
sentences.i: The wolf, meanwhile, had shoved and pulled and bitten at the stone, and worked away until he had freed his paws and drawn them out of the cleft again.
sentences.n: “indessen” = meanwhile. “gerückt” (rücken, to shove/budge), “gezogen” (ziehen), “gebissen” (beißen) are past participles. “frei gemacht” = made free, freed. This undoes the wolf’s trap from the first scene.

sentences.t: Voll Zorn und Wuth eilte er hinter dem Spielmann her, und wollte ihn zerreißen.
sentences.l: Full of anger and rage he hurried along after the musician, and wanted to tear him apart.
sentences.i: Full of anger and fury he hurried after the fiddler, meaning to tear him to pieces.
sentences.n: “Wuth” is the 1857 spelling of Wut (‘rage’), with the old -th-. “eilte ... hinter ... her” — hereilen, ‘hurried along after.’ “zerreißen” = to tear apart (prefix zer-, ‘to pieces’).

sentences.t: Als ihn der Fuchs laufen sah, fieng er an zu jammern, und schrie aus Leibeskräften „Bruder Wolf, komm mir zur Hilfe, der Spielmann hat mich betrogen.“
sentences.l: When the fox saw him running, he began to wail and cried with all his bodily strength, “Brother Wolf, come to my help, the musician has deceived me.”
sentences.i: When the fox saw him running by, he began to wail, and cried at the top of his lungs, “Brother Wolf, come to my help — the fiddler has tricked me!”
sentences.n: “fieng ... an” is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. “aus Leibeskräften” = with all one’s might (literally ‘out of body-strengths’). “komm mir zur Hilfe” = come to my aid (dative mir).

sentences.t: Der Wolf zog die Bäumchen herab, biß die Schnüre entzwei und machte den Fuchs frei, der mit ihm gieng und an dem Spielmann Rache nehmen wollte.
sentences.l: The wolf pulled the little trees down, bit the cords in two and made the fox free, who went with him and wanted to take revenge on the musician.
sentences.i: The wolf pulled the saplings down, bit the cords in two, and set the fox free, who went along with him, meaning to take revenge on the fiddler.
sentences.n: “zog ... herab” (herabziehen) is separable. “entzwei” = in two, apart. “an jemandem Rache nehmen” = to take revenge on someone.

sentences.t: Sie fanden das gebundene Häschen, das sie ebenfalls erlösten, und dann suchten alle zusammen ihren Feind auf.
sentences.l: They found the bound little hare, which they likewise freed, and then all together sought out their enemy.
sentences.i: They found the little hare still tied up, which they set free as well, and then all together went to seek out their enemy.
sentences.n: “gebundene” is the past participle of binden (‘to bind’) used as an adjective, ‘the bound (hare).’ “ebenfalls” = likewise, as well. “erlösten” (erlösen) = released, delivered. “suchten ... auf” (aufsuchen, to seek out) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Spielmann hatte auf seinem Weg abermals seine Fidel erklingen lassen, und diesmal war er glücklicher gewesen.
sentences.l: The musician had on his way once again let his fiddle ring out, and this time he had been luckier.
sentences.i: The fiddler had once again let his fiddle ring out along his way, and this time he had had better luck.
sentences.n: “Fidel” is another word for the fiddle (alongside Geige). “erklingen lassen” = to let (an instrument) ring out. “glücklicher” = luckier (comparative of glücklich).

sentences.t: Die Töne drangen zu den Ohren eines armen Holzhauers, der alsbald, er mochte wollen oder nicht, von der Arbeit abließ, und mit dem Beil unter dem Arme heran kam die Musik zu hören.
sentences.l: The tones pressed to the ears of a poor woodcutter, who at once, whether he would or not, left off from his work and came up with the axe under his arm to hear the music.
sentences.i: The notes reached the ears of a poor woodcutter, who at once, willing or not, left off his work and came up, hatchet under his arm, to hear the music.
sentences.n: “Töne” = tones, notes (plural of Ton). “drangen” (dringen) = pressed, penetrated. “alsbald” = at once, presently. “er mochte wollen oder nicht” = ‘whether he would or not.’ “abließ” (ablassen von, to leave off) is separable. “Beil” = hatchet, axe. “Holzhauers” is the genitive of Holzhauer (woodcutter).

sentences.t: „Endlich kommt doch der rechte Geselle,“ sagte der Spielmann, „denn einen Menschen suchte ich und keine wilden Thiere.“
sentences.l: “At last the right companion is coming after all,” said the musician, “for a human being I was seeking and no wild animals.”
sentences.i: “At last the right companion is coming,” said the fiddler, “for it was a man I was seeking, and no wild beasts.”
sentences.n: “doch” here = ‘after all, at last indeed.’ “Thiere” is the 1857 spelling of Tiere (‘animals’), with the old -th-. This is the point of the whole tale: the fiddler wanted human company, not the beasts he kept trapping.

sentences.t: Und fieng an und spielte so schön und lieblich, daß der arme Mann wie bezaubert da stand, und ihm das Herz vor Freude aufgieng.
sentences.l: And began and played so beautifully and sweetly that the poor man stood there as if enchanted, and his heart opened up to him for joy.
sentences.i: And he began and played so beautifully and sweetly that the poor man stood there as if spellbound, and his heart swelled with joy.
sentences.n: “fieng an” again (anfangen). “wie bezaubert” = as if enchanted (bezaubern, to bewitch). “ihm das Herz ... aufgieng” — dative ihm; “das Herz geht (einem) auf” is an idiom, ‘the heart opens/swells (with joy).’ “aufgieng” is the 1857 spelling of aufging (aufgehen).

sentences.t: Und wie er so stand, kamen der Wolf, der Fuchs und das Häslein heran, und er merkte wohl daß sie etwas Böses im Schilde führten.
sentences.l: And as he stood thus, the wolf, the fox and the little hare came up, and he noticed well that they had something evil in their shield.
sentences.i: And as he stood there, the wolf, the fox, and the little hare came up, and he saw plainly that they were up to no good.
sentences.n: “etwas Böses im Schilde führen” is a set idiom, ‘to be up to something (evil)’ — literally ‘to carry something on one’s shield,’ from heraldry. “merkte wohl” = noticed well, saw plainly.

sentences.t: Da erhob er seine blinkende Axt und stellte sich vor den Spielmann, als wollte er sagen „wer an ihn will, der hüte sich, der hat es mit mir zu thun.“
sentences.l: Then he raised his gleaming axe and placed himself before the musician, as if he wanted to say, “whoever wants at him, let him beware; he has it to do with me.”
sentences.i: Then he raised his gleaming axe and set himself in front of the fiddler, as if to say, “Whoever means harm to him had better beware — he has me to reckon with.”
sentences.n: “blinkende” (blinkend, from blinken) = gleaming, glinting. “stellte sich ... vor” (sich vor jemanden stellen) — placed himself before. “wer an ihn will” = ‘whoever wants at him,’ i.e. means to attack him. “der hüte sich” = ‘let him beware’ (a subjunctive of command, sich hüten). “es mit jemandem zu thun haben” = to have to deal/reckon with someone (thun = tun).

sentences.t: Da ward den Thieren angst und liefen in den Wald zurück, der Spielmann aber spielte dem Manne noch eins zum Dank und zog dann weiter.
sentences.l: Then the animals grew afraid and ran back into the wood; but the musician played the man one more tune in thanks and then moved on further.
sentences.i: Then the beasts grew afraid and ran back into the wood; but the fiddler played the man one more tune in thanks, and then went on his way.
sentences.n: “ward den Thieren angst” — an impersonal dative idiom, ‘the animals became afraid’ (jemandem wird angst); “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden. “spielte ... noch eins” — “eins” again for ‘one more tune,’ echoing “fidelte eins” at the very start. “zog ... weiter” (weiterziehen, to move on) is separable.
